exercises.title: The five pieces
exercises.prompt: Name the five components of a Stable Diffusion pipeline and what each does in one line: VAE, text encoder, U-Net, scheduler, safety checker.
exercises.hint: VAE compresses/decodes latents; text encoder (CLIP/T5) turns prompt into embeddings; U-Net denoises latents conditioned on text; scheduler defines the sampling steps; safety checker filters output.
exercises.solution:
- **VAE:** encodes images to a small latent (and decodes latents back to pixels) so diffusion runs cheaply in latent space.
- **Text encoder (CLIP/T5):** turns the prompt into token embeddings the U-Net can attend to.
- **U-Net:** the denoiser — predicts noise in the latent at each step, conditioned on the text embeddings via cross-attention.
- **Scheduler (sampler):** defines the noise schedule and how many/large the reverse steps are (DDIM, DPM++, etc.).
- **Safety checker:** post-hoc filter on generated images.

Text-to-image = encode prompt → denoise a random latent over N scheduler steps with the U-Net → VAE-decode to pixels.

exercises.title: Why latent diffusion is ~48× cheaper
exercises.prompt: Explain why running diffusion in a 4×64×64 latent instead of a 3×512×512 image cuts compute roughly 48× without much quality loss. What makes this possible?
exercises.hint: Compare element counts: 3·512·512 vs 4·64·64. The VAE handles perceptual detail; diffusion only models semantic structure in the latent.
exercises.solution:
```python
pix = 3*512*512      # 786432
lat = 4*64*64        # 16384
print(pix/lat)       # ~48x fewer values per step
```

Every U-Net forward pass (and there are dozens per image) processes ~48× fewer values in the latent, and that factor dominates both training and sampling cost. It works because a **pretrained VAE** already captures high-frequency perceptual detail (textures, sharp edges) in its decoder, so the diffusion model only has to learn the **semantic/structural** content in the compact latent — the part that actually needs a powerful generative model. Splitting 'perceptual compression' (VAE, trained once) from 'semantic generation' (diffusion, in latent space) is the key idea that made high-res text-to-image feasible on consumer GPUs.

exercises.title: LoRA fine-tuning + CFG
exercises.prompt: Explain how LoRA lets you fine-tune Stable Diffusion on a small custom dataset cheaply, and what classifier-free guidance (CFG) does at inference. Why are both so widely used?
exercises.hint: LoRA: train small low-rank deltas on attention weights, freeze the base. CFG: mix conditional and unconditional predictions to amplify prompt adherence.
exercises.solution:
**LoRA** freezes the full U-Net and inserts small **low-rank weight deltas** (`W + BA`, with B,A tiny) into the attention layers, training only those. For a few dozen images of a new style/subject you train ~megabytes of adapter weights in minutes on one GPU, then load the adapter at inference (and stack/swap multiple). It avoids storing/retraining the whole multi-GB model per concept — the reason there are thousands of community LoRAs.

**Classifier-free guidance** runs the U-Net twice per step — once **with** the text condition, once **unconditional** — and extrapolates: `pred = uncond + scale·(cond − uncond)`. Raising the guidance scale pushes samples to follow the prompt more strongly (sharper adherence, less diversity; too high → over-saturated artifacts). It needs no separate classifier (the model is trained with occasional null prompts to learn the unconditional path).

Both are ubiquitous because they're cheap levers on a frozen base model: LoRA customizes *what* it generates, CFG controls *how strongly* it obeys the prompt — together they turn one pretrained checkpoint into an endlessly steerable tool.
